subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Emission Nebulae: Glowing Gas
description: A 60-second lesson on Emission Nebulae: Glowing Gas.

hook.text: Emission nebulae are vast clouds of gas that glow in vivid colors, lit up by ultraviolet radiation from nearby hot stars.

buildup.text: The UV light ionizes hydrogen atoms. When electrons recombine, they emit light—often the famous red hydrogen-alpha glow.

discovery.text: The Orion Nebula is the most famous—visible to the naked eye as a fuzzy patch in Orion's sword, 1,344 light-years away.

twist.text: These nebulae are stellar nurseries. Inside them, dense clumps of gas collapse under gravity to form new stars.

climax.text: JWST has peered deep into the Orion Nebula, revealing young planetary systems forming inside dusty cocoons.

punchline.text: Glowing clouds of star-forming gas. The universe's most beautiful factories.

quiz.question: What makes emission nebulae glow?
options: Ultraviolet radiation from nearby stars ionizes gas, which emits light when electrons recombine
They reflect light from distant galaxies like a cosmic mirror
Gravitational energy from collapsing gas converts directly into visible light
